code.description: Code generation, debugging, algorithm design, refactoring, and software engineering tasks.
code.evidenceExpectations: Responses must demonstrate concrete, executable logic. Syntactically valid and runnable code snippets are the primary evidence type. Vague or pseudocode-only responses are insufficient.

research.description: Explanatory, historical, or inquiry-based tasks requiring factual accuracy and source discipline.
research.evidenceExpectations: Factual claims must be traceable to authoritative sources. Responses should acknowledge gaps and uncertainty rather than speculate beyond available evidence.

analysis.description: Evaluative, comparative, or quantitative tasks requiring structured reasoning and evidence-backed conclusions.
analysis.evidenceExpectations: Evaluative conclusions must be supported by quantitative or qualitative evidence. Opinions stated without supporting data are treated as low-confidence assertions.

creative.description: Fiction, poetry, narrative, screenplay, and artistic composition tasks.
creative.evidenceExpectations: Creative coherence and originality are the primary quality signals. Factual accuracy requirements are relaxed for fictional content; narrative quality and adherence to the user brief take precedence.

marketing.description: Persuasive content, brand messaging, campaign copy, and audience-oriented communication tasks.
marketing.evidenceExpectations: Claims about products, services, or audiences must align with brand guidelines. Persuasive language is appropriate; unsupported factual assertions about competitors or market share are not.

general.description: Tasks that do not match a specific domain, or multi-domain and ambiguous prompts.
general.evidenceExpectations: No domain-specific evidence standards apply. General accuracy, clarity, and logical coherence are the primary quality signals.
